section.title: Introduction
section.p: The JUnitXML module provides an interface for generating JUnit-compatible XML test result files on RISC OS systems. It allows test frameworks and test runners to record test results in a standardised format that can be consumed by continuous integration systems, test reporting tools, and other software development utilities.

The module implements a SWI-based interface for creating test suites, recording test cases with their outcomes (success, failure, error, or skipped), and generating XML output conforming to the JUnit XML schema.

item.p: Support for multiple test suites within a single output file
Flexible timestamp handling (current time, RISC OS time, Unix time, or ISO 8601 strings)
Package and class name support for organised test hierarchies
Detailed failure and error reporting with type and message
Duration tracking for test suites and individual test cases

section.title: Technical Details
section.p: The JUnitXML module maintains internal state for each open handle, tracking test suites and test cases as they are created. The module uses a handle-based API where each handle represents an independent XML output stream.

subsection.title: Memory Management
subsection.p: The module allocates memory dynamically for:

item.p: Test suite structures and their properties

subsection.p: All memory is freed when a handle is closed or when the module is terminated. The module maintains a linked list of handles to allow multiple concurrent XML output streams.

subsection.title: Timestamp Handling
subsection.p: The module supports multiple timestamp formats for test suite creation:

item.p: Current time: Automatically uses the current system time
RISC OS time: Accepts a RISC OS 5-byte quin (centiseconds since 1900-01-01)
Unix time: Accepts a Unix epoch timestamp (seconds since 1970-01-01)
ISO 8601: Accepts a pre-formatted ISO 8601 timestamp string

subsection.p: RISC OS time values are converted to ISO 8601 format using the Territory_ConvertDateAndTime SWI for local time conversion, with manual adjustment for UTC epoch differences.

subsection.title: XML Output
subsection.p: The module generates JUnit-compatible XML output with the following structure:

item.p: Root testsuites element with aggregate statistics
Individual testsuite elements with properties and test cases
Test case elements with optional failure or error child elements

subsection.p: The XML is written incrementally as test cases are closed, reducing memory usage for large test suites.

entry.register-use:
Flags
If set, R1 points to a filename string
Reserved
Filename pointer (if bit 0 of flags set)

use.p: JUnitXML_Create creates a new JUnit XML output handle. The handle can be used to create test suites and record test results.
Flags:

item.p: Bit 0 (JUnitXML_Create_FilenameGiven): If set, R1 points to a filename string where the XML output will be written. If clear, the module will generate a default filename.

use.p: The function returns a positive handle ID on success, or a negative error code on failure.

entry.register-use:
Flags
Operation code: 0=Create, 1=Close, 2=Update, 3=Property
If set, R4 points to a package name
Timestamp format: 0=Current, 1=RISC OS, 2=Unix, 3=ISO 8601
If set, duration is supplied
Reserved

use.p: JUnitXML_TestSuite performs operations on test suites. The operation is determined by bits 0-3 of the flags word.
Operation Create (0): Creates a new test suite within the handle.
Flags for Create:

item.p: Bit 4 (JUnitXML_TestSuite_PackageSupplied): If set, R4 points to a package name

use.p: Parameters for Create:

item.p: R2 = ID string pointer (or NULL for auto-generated)
R4 = Package string pointer (if bit 4 set, else ignored)
Format 1 (JUNIT_TS_RISCOS): Pointer to 5-byte RISC OS time quin

use.p: Operation Close (1): Closes the current test suite, writing final statistics.
Flags for Close:

item.p: Bit 7 (JUnitXML_TestSuite_DurationPresent): If set, duration is supplied

use.p: Parameters for Close:

item.p: R2 = Duration in centiseconds (if bit 7 set, else ignored)

use.p: Operation Property (3): Sets a property on the current test suite.
Parameters for Property:

example.p: Creating a test suite with current timestamp:

p.extended-example:
MOV R0, #0 ; flags = Create, current time
MOV R1, handle ; handle ID
LDR R2, =suite_id ; suite ID string
LDR R3, =suite_name ; suite name string
SWI "JUnitXML_TestSuite"

example.p: Closing a test suite with duration:

p.extended-example:
MOV R0, #1 ORR (128) ; flags = Close, duration present
MOV R1, handle ; handle ID
MOV R2, #100 ; duration = 100 centiseconds
SWI "JUnitXML_TestSuite"

entry.register-use:
Flags
Operation code: 0=Create, 1=Close, 2=Update
Test status: 0=None, 1=Success, 2=Failure, 3=Error, 4=Skipped
If set, failure_type is a RISC OS error block
Reserved

use.p: JUnitXML_TestCase performs operations on test cases. The operation is determined by bits 0-3 of the flags word.
Operation Create (0): Creates a new test case within the current suite.
Flags for Create:

item.p: Bit 8 (JUnitXML_TestCase_ErrorBlock): If set, failure_type is a RISC OS error block

use.p: Status codes:
Parameters for Create:

item.p: R2 = ID string pointer (or NULL for auto-generated)
R5 = Failure type string pointer (or NULL)
R6 = Failure message string pointer (or NULL)

use.p: Operation Close (1): Closes the current test case, writing it to the output.
Parameters for Close:

p.extended-example:
MOV R0, #16 ; flags = Create, status = SUCCESS (1 << 4)
MOV R1, handle ; handle ID
LDR R2, =test_id ; test ID string
LDR R3, =classname ; class name string
LDR R4, =test_name ; test name string
MOV R5, #0 ; no failure type
MOV R6, #0 ; no failure message
SWI "JUnitXML_TestCase"

MOV R0, #32 ; flags = Create, status = FAILURE (2 << 4)
MOV R1, handle ; handle ID
LDR R2, =test_id ; test ID string
LDR R3, =classname ; class name string
LDR R4, =test_name ; test name string
LDR R5, =fail_type ; "AssertionError"
LDR R6, =fail_msg ; "Expected 5 but got 3"
SWI "JUnitXML_TestCase"

example.p: Closing a test case with duration:

p.extended-example:
MOV R0, #1 ; flags = Close
MOV R1, handle ; handle ID
MOV R2, #50 ; duration = 50 centiseconds
SWI "JUnitXML_TestCase"

entry.register-use:
Flags
If set, R1 points to an output filename
Reserved

use.p: JUnitXML_Close closes a JUnit XML handle, writing the final XML structure and freeing resources. The handle is removed from the internal handle list.
Flags:

item.p: Bit 0 (JUnitXML_Close_FilenameGiven): If set, R1 points to an output filename. This overrides the filename specified during handle creation.

use.p: The function closes the most recently created handle if multiple handles exist. For precise control, ensure handles are closed in reverse order of creation.

example.p: Closing a handle with the original filename:

p.extended-example:
MOV R0, #0 ; flags = use original filename
SWI "JUnitXML_Close"

example.p: Closing a handle with a different filename:

p.extended-example:
MOV R0, #1 ; flags = filename given
LDR R1, =newname ; new filename string
SWI "JUnitXML_Close"

use.p: JUnitXML_Result returns aggregate result counts for all test suites recorded within the given handle. The counts are summed across all suites, including suites that are still open.
The number of passes is derived as the total test count minus failures, errors, and skips.

example.p: Reading result counts after running tests:

p.extended-example:
SYS "JUnitXML_Result", 0, jx% TO tests%, passes%, failures%, errors%, skips%
PRINT "Tests: "; tests%
PRINT "Passes: "; passes%
PRINT "Failures: "; failures%

section.title: Error Messages
section.p: The JUnitXML module returns error blocks in the standard RISC OS format. The error numbers are module-specific with base &822A00, and are returned as negative values from SWI calls or via the carry flag.

use.p: Module initialisation failed. The state manager could not be initialised.
Failed to create a new JUnit XML handle. This may be due to memory exhaustion or invalid parameters.
Failed to create a test suite within the handle. This may occur if the handle is invalid or if memory allocation fails.
Failed to close a test suite. This may occur if the suite is not properly formed or if writing to the output file fails.
An invalid operation code was specified for JUnitXML_TestSuite. The operation must be 0 (Create), 1 (Close), or 3 (Property).
Failed to set a property on a test suite. This may occur if the property name or value is invalid, or if memory allocation fails.
Failed to create a test case. This may occur if the handle or current suite is invalid, or if memory allocation fails.
Failed to close a test case. This may occur if writing to the output file fails.
An invalid operation code was specified for JUnitXML_TestCase. The operation must be 0 (Create) or 1 (Close).
Failed to close a JUnit XML handle. This may occur if writing the final XML structure fails or if the handle is invalid.
No handle is available to close. All handles may have already been closed, or the module may not have been initialised.

section.title: Usage
section.p: The JUnitXML module is loaded automatically when a program links against it, or it can be loaded manually using the RMEnsure command.

subsection.title: Loading the Module
subsection.p: The module will be loaded from the usual module search path. Once loaded, the SWI calls described above can be used to create and manage JUnit XML output.

subsection.title: Typical Usage Pattern
subsection.p: A typical usage pattern for generating JUnit XML output:

item.p: Call JUnitXML_Create to obtain a handle
Call JUnitXML_TestSuite with operation Create to start a test suite
Call JUnitXML_TestSuite with operation Close to finish the test suite
Call JUnitXML_Close to close the handle and write the final XML

subsection.title: Unloading the Module
subsection.p: All open handles will be closed and their resources freed when the module is unloaded.

section.title: Compatibility
section.p: The JUnitXML module is compatible with:

item.p: RISC OS 5 and later (32-bit and 64-bit builds)
RPCEmu and other RISC OS emulators

section.p: The module requires the following SWIs to be available:

subsection.title: Complete Example in C
subsection.p: This example shows how to create a simple JUnit XML file with one test suite containing three test cases:

subsection.title: Example Output
subsection.p: The above C code would generate XML similar to:

p.extended-example:
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<testsuites>
<testsuite name="MyTestSuite" package=""
id="suite1" tests="2" failures="1" errors="0" skipped="0"
time="1.00" timestamp="2026-03-27T10:30:00Z">
<testcase id="test1" classname="MyTestClass" name="test_addition"
time="0.25"/>
<testcase id="test2" classname="MyTestClass" name="test_subtraction"
time="0.30">
<failure type="AssertionError">Expected 3 but got 4</failure>
</testcase>
</testsuite>
</testsuites>

disclaimer.p: Copyright © 2026 Charles Ferguson. All rights reserved.
This documentation is provided "as is" without warranty of any kind.

revision.author: CF
revision.title: Initial version
revision.change: Created initial PRM-in-XML documentation for JUnitXML module

revision.author: CF
revision.title: Add JUnitXML_Result SWI
revision.change: Added documentation for JUnitXML_Result SWI which returns aggregate test result counts
